coredata.dc:title: Preparation of full-color carbon quantum dots with multiple emission centers
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, XingYuan
Li, JianFeng
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon dots have attracted more and more attention due to their unique advantages, but there has been a great controversy about the study of their mechanism. A clear mechanism is of great importance for the structural design of carbon dots. To investigate the photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots, full-color carbon dots with multiple emission centers were successfully prepared by using citric acid and water-soluble aniline blue. It is shown that the red shift of emission wavelength is attributed to the synergistic effect of N and O-related surface functional groups and quantum size. The successful preparation of cold white light diodes from mixed trichromatic carbon dots indicates that this type of carbon dot has important applications in light-emitting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
Fluorescence
Synergistic effect
Optoelectronic devices

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Fujian Bureau of Major Projects (2019H4018) funding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Citric acid and water-soluble aniline blue were used as raw materials to prepare F-CDs; (b) Normalized PL emission spectra of all the above samples; The corresponding UV–vis, PL emission, and PL excitation spectra of B-CDs (c), G-CDs (d), and R-CDs (e).
Diagram of the possible mechanism of surface state transfer to core state.

TEM images of B-CDs(a), G-CDs(b), and R-CDs(c). The following corresponds to its HRTEM image and size distribution; (d) FT-IR spectra of the three selected CDs;(e) XRD patterns of the three selected CDs;(f) XPS full spectrum scans of the three CDs.
High resolution XPS spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs.

Fluorescence imaging photographs of the CDs/PVA composite film at 365 nm are (a1) B-CDs, (b1) G-CDs, (c1) R-CDs and (d1) W-CDs, respectively; a2-d2 are the corresponding CIE diagrams; a3-d3 are the corresponding LED diagrams.

head.ce:title: Preparation of full-color carbon quantum dots with multiple emission centers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xian, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon dots have attracted more and more attention due to their unique advantages, but there has been a great controversy about the study of their mechanism. A clear mechanism is of great importance for the structural design of carbon dots. To investigate the photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots, full-color carbon dots with multiple emission centers were successfully prepared by using citric acid and water-soluble aniline blue. It is shown that the red shift of emission wavelength is attributed to the synergistic effect of N and O-related surface functional groups and quantum size. The successful preparation of cold white light diodes from mixed trichromatic carbon dots indicates that this type of carbon dot has important applications in light-emitting devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new type of photoluminescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of good water solubility, tunable emission wavelength, nontoxic, easy to modify and functionalize, etc. They are widely used in optoelectronic devices, bio-imaging, fluorescence detection, photocatalysis, antibacterial and other fields [1–6]. Among the many merits of CDs, the tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties at a single excitation wavelength, i.e., full-color carbon dots (F-CDs), have been widely studied [7]. Compared with the top-down approach, the bottom-up approach can prepare CDs with stronger fluorescence properties, especially with high PL quantum yields (QY) in the low-energy region, from more raw materials and different reaction conditions, and is therefore adopted by more research. The wide range of preparation materials and numerous preparation methods lead to a wide variety of properties of the prepared CDs, therefore, it is essential to deeply understand the relationship between the complex structure/composition of CDs and the preparation materials and methods [8]. In most of the studies, the reactions were performed under high temperature and pressure conditions. While the method certainly has a very important influence on the fluorescence properties of F-CDs, However, the selection of the appropriate material will directly determine the outcome of the study.

The selection of raw materials plays a decisive role in the preparation of CDs, and different raw materials can be used to prepare CDs with different PL properties [9,10]. Currently, the preparation of F-CDs is divided into two main categories: organic small molecule materials and aromatic organic molecule materials. The organic small molecule materials are mainly CDs prepared from citric acid and various nitrogen-containing compounds. Citric acid and urea were used as the best raw material pair to prepare F-CDs with excellent performance under several reaction conditions [11–13]. In addition, F-CDs were also successfully prepared by changing the nitrogen-containing elements [14]. Aromatic organic molecules containing luminescent units and abundant functional groups are considered another ideal material for the preparation of CDs [9]. Typically, Lin et al. successfully prepared F-CDs from three isomers of phenylenediamine, with PL emission variations caused by differences in their particle size and nitrogen content [15]. In addition, the preparation of high-quality F-CDs from a single o-phenylenediamine under different reaction conditions has greatly facilitated the preparation of many aromatic molecules [16,17]. Numerous studies have shown that the benzene ring structure contained in aromatic molecules facilitates the increase in the size of CDs, resulting in a narrower energy band gap and red-shifted emission wavelength [18–20].

The combination of organic and aromatic small-molecule materials is a valuable area of research. One popular approach is to mix citric acid/amino acids, which have rich functional groups, with aromatic molecules to create F-CDs [21–23]. However, as research progresses, the mechanisms behind F-CDs become less clear and even contradictory. It is crucial to have an accurate understanding of the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism in order to synthesize F-CDs with tunable PL properties using a convenient and efficient strategy. Unfortunately, this has proven to be a challenging task. While conventional raw materials containing nitrogen and benzene ring structures have been used to prepare F-CDs by varying reaction conditions, there have been few reports on the preparation of F-CDs using dyes.

Here, a new material with multiple benzene ring structures and containing nitrogen was used to synthesize F-CDs. F-CDs covering the entire visible range (465–650 nm) were prepared by a hot solvent method using citric acid and water-soluble aniline blue as raw materials. excitation spectra showed that F-CDs had multiple emission centers, which gradually shifted from the surface state to the core state with the red shift of the emission wavelength. The quantum yields of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs were 14%, 25.2% and 30%, respectively. The flexible fluorescent film was successfully prepared by mixing with PVA, and the three-primary color carbon dots were mixed to successfully prepare white LED with cold light, which indicates that the prepared CDs have important applications in the fields of flexible display and optoelectronic devices. Ideally, white LEDs made by combining UV LED chips with B-, G- and R-CDs phosphors can meet the needs of indoor and outdoor lighting and plant photosynthesis, and more importantly, can reduce the toxicity of blue light.

CA were purchased from the Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). polyvinyl alcohol (Mw=96500), and concentrated sulfuric acid (98 wt%) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Water-soluble aniline blue were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Organic solvents (ethanol and petroleum ether) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All experimental water is distilled water. All chemical reagents and solvents are used directly as they are without further purification.

The F-CDs were prepared by reacting citric acid and water-soluble aniline blue in different solvents. 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.007 g of water-soluble aniline blue were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and ultrasonically dispersed for ten minutes, the mixture was then transferred into a 20 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for heating 6 h at 200 °C. After the reaction was cooled to room temperature, the original solution was poured out and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the insoluble particles and impurities generated by carbonation. The resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μ m membrane filter to remove large particles. The B-CDs with an emission wavelength of 465 nm was prepared, and the 475 nm B-CDs and 500 nm G-CDs only changed the solvent to 0.7 mol/L dilute sulfuric acid solution and 1.0 mol/L mixture of dilute sulfuric acid and ethanol (1:1/v:v). For the yellow-green, yellow, orange and red CDs only the reaction solvent was changed to a mixture of ethanol and deionized water (1:1 v/v). The latter four colors were prepared by silica gel column chromatography using a mixture of petroleum ether and anhydrous ethanol (10:1/v:v) as the unfolding agent to separate the original solutions. The solutions used were sorted and stored for further characterization and analysis.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the morphological distribution and size of CDs using JEOL JEM 2100 F. Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA, was used to analyze the CDs for XPS detection and to analyze the elemental composition of the CDs. Horiba LabRAM HR, Japan, was used to analyze CDs for Raman testing to analyze the degree of graphitization, which was not expanded in the paper because the fluorescence of R-CDs was too strong to be measured. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. PL measurements were tested by RF-5301PC (SHIMADZU, Japan). Both the excitation and emission slits were set as 3 and 3 nm, respectively. Ultraviolet−visible (UV−Vis) spectra were obtained on an UV−Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1900, Purkinje General, China). The crystallinity of the CDs was analyzed using a D/max2200PC X-ray diffractometer with a scan rate of 6°/min. All the fluorescence spectra were processed by using normalized method through dividing each of fluorescence intensity of PL spectra by the maximum value of their own.

For the blue/green /red florescent films, 2 mL of the corresponding CDs solution (10 mg·mL−1) was mixed with 20 mL of PVA aqueous solution (0.50 g·mL−1), and then dropped on a cleaned glass sheet followed by drying for 6 h under Indoor Environment.

The mixed solution for the preparation of carbon dot-based fluorescent PVA film was carefully suspended on a chip of 365 nm UV beads, dried at 60 °C for four hours, and the same operation was repeated three times.

F-CDs were prepared by using citric acid and water-soluble aniline blue containing multiple benzene ring structures in the molecular structure in water, dilute sulfuric acid and/or ethanol solutions. The synthesis process diagram, the color under sunlight and the tunable fluorescence diagram from blue to red under 365 nm UV lamp are shown in Fig. 1(a). The best fluorescence emission peaks were located at 465 nm, 475 nm, 500 nm, 560 nm, 580 nm, 600 and 650 nm, covering the whole visible range, and the normalized fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 1(b). Three samples with different colors and similar intensities blue (475 nm), green (500 nm), and red (650 nm) were selected and labeled as B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively, for further characterization.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra, excitation spectra and emission spectra of the three selected characteristic CDs are shown in Fig. 1(c)-(e). The UV absorption spectra of the three selected CDs samples all showed significant absorption peaks at 260 nm, corresponding to the π–π * transition of the aromatic sp2 domain CC and CN [1,2]. It is noteworthy that the B-CDs at 353 nm, the G-CDs at 362 nm and the R-CDs at 551 nm all have obvious absorption peaks, corresponding to the n–π * transition of CO on the surface of the CDs. The gradual increase in the intensity of absorbance from B-CDs to R-CDs indicates that the R-CDs contains more CO groups. More importantly, the enhancement of absorbance in the visible range is beneficial for the redshift of the emission wavelength of the CDs [3].

The excitation spectra of the three characteristic samples are shown as the black lines in Fig. 1(c)-(e). It can be clearly observed that the B-CDs has two best excitation peaks (at 352 nm and 410 nm, respectively) and multiple shoulders, the G-CDs has two significant excitation peaks (at 335 nm and 445 nm, respectively) and one shoulder, and the R-CDs has three characteristic excitation peaks (at 337 nm, 475 and 620 nm, respectively). The B-CDs and R-CDs colors show excitation-independent emission properties, while the G-CDs shows excitation-dependent emission properties, probably due to the presence of dilute sulfuric acid and ethanol, which makes the G-CDs more deeply dehydrated and carbonized, forming more characteristic structures with more specific surface states (Supporting Information Fig. S1). To investigate the phenomenon of multiple excitation peaks for three samples, time-resolved PL decay spectroscopy tests were performed on three samples. The results show that a double exponential formulation can be fitted to the spectral peaks, including the short-lived component τ1 (∼3 ns) and the long-lived component τ2 (∼10 ns), which are attributed to the core and surface states of the radiative composite process, respectively (Supporting Information Fig. S2; Table S1). From B-CDs to G-CDs and then to R-CDs, the average lifetime increases first from 24.19 ns to 117.77 ns and then decreases to 2.88 ns. Significantly, the average fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs reaches more than one hundred, which may be produced by the special structure due to the severe reaction conditions. More importantly, the percentage of τ1 increases from 8.90% to 96.77% and the percentage of τ2 decreases from 91.10% to 3.23% from B-CDs to R-CDs, indicates that the radiative lifetime effect of CDs shifts from surface to core state as the emission wavelength red-shifts.

B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs were synthesized using the same starting materials by Ghorai et al. For the first time, the excited-state carrier dynamics of different emitting B-Dots were explored and the source of fluorescence from them was found using steady-state, ultrafast time-resolved absorption and emission techniques. The results show that all CDs contain core states and surface states [4]. The assignment of the surface and core states of the CDs was investigated by Qu et al. They attributed the optical jump associated with the yellow/green emission to the optical center in the core state of the CDs and the red emission to the surface emission center of the CDs because the latter is sensitive to solvent, temperature and UV [5]. Therefore, it is reasonable to speculate that the three characteristic samples have multiple significant excitation peaks, which are mainly attributed to the formation of multiple luminescence centers in the core and surface states of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2, from B-CDs to R-CDs, the optimal excitation wavelength gradually increases with the red-shift of the emission wavelength, and the luminescence center shifts from the surface state to the core state. The relative PL QY of CDs were calculated to be 14% for B-CDs, 25.2% for G-CDs, and 30% for R-CDs.

In order to understand the structure and chemical composition of the CDs, a series of characterizations were carried out. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs are shown in Fig. 3(a)-(c), respectively. The three types of CDs do not have large differences in size and are evenly dispersed without agglomeration. The average particle sizes of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs were 2.11 ± 0.63, 2.34 ± 0.54 and 2.47 ± 0.37 nm, respectively. With the increase of CDs particle size, the emission wavelength is gradually red-shifted, which is consistent with the findings in other works and proves that the size effect is the main factor affecting the red-shift of emission wavelength in this work. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the three types of CDs show that all types have high-resolution lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which originates from the (100) planes of graphite carbon. This implies that the CDs we prepared contain highly crystalline graphene-like conjugated structures [6]. The thermal stability of B-, G-, and R-CDs was investigated using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (supporting information Fig. S3). The samples showed a small mass loss below 100 °C, which may be due to the desorption of trace amounts of adsorbed water in the samples. A larger loss of sample mass occurred between 230 °C and 310 °C, which could be caused by the decomposition of surface N and O-related groups in the CDs; the mass loss in the range of 310–420 °C was attributed to the decomposition of graphitized carbon nuclei. In the TGA analysis, R-CDs showed greater mass loss than both B-CDs and G-CDs, and the above characterization studies suggest that both B-, G-, and R-CDs are rich in polar functional groups and that R-CDs contain more surface groups than B-CDs and G-CDs.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra are shown in Fig. 3(d). Typically, OH at 3642 cm−1, NH2 at 3245 cm−1, 1080 cm−1/1123 cm−1 is C-O, especially the peak at 1650 cm−1 corresponds to CC/CO, and the intensity increases slightly from B-CDs to R-CDs, combined with XRD inferred as CO peak, indicating a gradual increase in oxidation from B-CDs to R-CDs. Up to this point, it can be inferred that CO has a crucial role in the emission wavelength redshift [7,8]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the three types of CDs are shown in Fig. 3(e). From B-CDs to R-CDs all have intense diffraction peaks at 21.5° with a gradual increase in intensity, corresponding to graphene with a layer spacing of 0.41 nm, which is mainly due to two factors: the oxygen-containing functional groups are likely embedded in the graphene layers during the dehydration-carbonation reaction, resulting in a wider graphene spacing [1,3]; it is also possible that the reaction temperature is too high or the air pressure is too high, resulting in distortion and twisting of sp3C in the graphene planes [9]. The intensity gradually increases from B-CDs to R-CDs, and also the degree of surface graphitization increases, which could not be verified using Raman because the R-CDs fluoresced too strongly [10]. In particular, the R-CDs have a strong peak at 12.6°, corresponding to the (001) crystal plane of graphene oxide [5]. Thus, the XRD results amply indicate a gradual increase of oxidation from B-CDs to R-CDs.

To verify the above conclusions, the surface groups of three typical CDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS full spectrum scans of the three CDs showed that all of them contained C, N, O elements in Fig. 3(f). The high-resolution elemental analysis of the F-CDs is shown in Fig. 4. In the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of all three CDs, The C1s spectra can be fitted into 4 kinds of C species sp2C (CC/C-C) at 284.7 eV, sp3C (C-O and C-N) at 285.6 eV, CO at 288.7 eV. It clearly shows a significant increase from 5.63% to 11.69% in the CO group from B-CDs to R-CDs (Supporting Information Table S3). For N1s, XPS spectra can be converted to Amino N (399.8 eV) and Pyrollic N (400.2 eV). The relative content of Amino N increases from B-CDs to R-CDs and the content of Pyrollic N decreases significantly from B-CDs to R-CDs (Supporting Information Table S4). The high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s contain two components associated with CO at 531.7 eV and C-O at 533.4 eV. There was a significant increase (from 69.62% to 83.77%) in the number of CO from B-CDs to R-CDs (Supporting Information Table S4). Combined with the fitting results of the carbon and oxygen peaks, it fully illustrates the red shift of the emission wavelength with increasing CO.

The properties of CDs are derived from their elemental composition and structural distribution. The elemental N content is 3.06% for B-CDs, 5.83% for G-CDs, and 3.61% for R-CDs (Supporting Information Table S2). It can be observed that with the red-shift of the emission wavelength, the content of elemental N shows an overall increasing trend, and the analysis combined with the supporting information in Table S3 and supporting information Table S4 shows that the increased N content mainly exists in the situation of C-N, amide N. Combining all the above results, it can be consistently concluded that the increase in the content of N and O-related functional groups leads to the narrowing of the energy band gap and the red-shift of the emission wavelength, which is the same as the results of many studies [2,3,9]. Combined with the previous TEM results, it can be inferred that the redshift of the emission wavelength in this work is mainly attributed to the synergistic effect of N and O-related surface states and size effects. The narrowing of the CDs energy band gap usually results in a red-shift of the emission wavelength. This can be verified by a calculation using the following equation:
(1) E g(opt) = 1240/ λ edge
where λedge is the wavelength of the maximum absorption edge. The Eg of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs are calculated to be 2.61 eV, 2.52 eV, and 1.87 eV, respectively, indicating that the redshift of the emission wavelength is related to the band gap. The relevant representative studies of F-CDs are summarized in the supporting information. S5, and it can be observed that the fluorescence we prepared spans a relatively large color range, basically covering the visible spectral range, and then has a relatively high QY.

Given the excellent fluorescence properties of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs in solution, the preparation of three carbon dots with PVA films was first attempted. Under daylight, these three composite films were homogeneous and transparent. Under UV lamp irradiation, all three composite fluorescent films emitted bright fluorescence with emission peaks located at 475 nm, 500, and 650 nm, which were consistent with the positions of the peaks appearing in the original CDs solution. White light-emitting carbon dots (W-CDs) were successfully prepared by mixing the three colors. The full-color fluorescent films are shown in Fig. 5(a1)-(d1). In addition to this, the panchromatic LEDs were fabricated by coating PVA composites containing the appropriate mass ratio of CDs on a 365 nm chip, as shown in Fig. 5(a3)-(d3). The prepared WLEDs had a correlated color temperature (CCT) w of 6553 K, a color rendering index of 82.5, and showed cool white light. The results show that F-CDs have good and wide application prospects in both fluorescent composites and optoelectronic devices.

In conclusion, F-CDs with multiple luminescent centers were synthesized using citric acid and water-soluble aniline blue containing multiple benzene ring structures. TEM, FT-IR, XRD, and XPS tests showed that the red-shift of the emission wavelength was mainly caused by the combination of the increase of N and O-related functional group content and the gradual increase of CD size. Finally, the prepared F-CDs were tried in composite thin films and photovoltaics, both with good results. This indicates that the prepared F-CDs have promising applications.
Jianfeng Li: Experimental manipulation, testing, first draft writing. XingYuan Ma: graph drawing, analysis, revising the article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Fujian Bureau of Major Projects (2019H4018) funding.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2023.106561.

ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cao M., Zhao X., Gong X. Ionic Liquid‐Assisted Fast Synthesis of Carbon Dots with Strong Fluorescence and Their Tunable Multicolor Emission[J]. Small, 2022, 18(11): 2106683.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvent‐controlled synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots with a wide color gamut and narrowed emission peak widths[J]

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ding H., Wei J.S., Zhang P., et al. Solvent‐controlled synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots with a wide color gamut and narrowed emission peak widths[J]. Small, 2018, 14(22): 1800612.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism[J]

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ding H., Yu S.B., Wei J.S., et al. Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism[J]. ACS nano, 2016, 10(1): 484–491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor carbon dots prepared by single‐factor control of graphitization and surface oxidation for high‐quality white light‐emitting diodes[J]

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng Y., Arkin K., Hao J., et al. Multicolor carbon dots prepared by single‐factor control of graphitization and surface oxidation for high‐quality white light‐emitting diodes[J]. Advanced Optical Materials, 2021, 9(19): 2100688.
